@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/sensory processing / vestibular system / movement seeking / self-regulation/vestibular seeking / inverted position preference / proprioceptive-vestibular integration / sensory modulation/9-materials-that-help-when-child-hangs-upside-down#faq

mainEntity.name:
Why does my daughter is five years old, and she hangs upside down constantly. i mean constantly. not occasionally, not sometimes — it's like a compulsion. she hangs off the couch with her head touching the floor, her legs draped over the cushions. she flips over the arm of the recliner and stays there, watching tv upside down. she lies across chairs with her head dangling off the edge. any piece of furniture becomes an opportunity to invert herself.

at the playground, she's not interested in the slides or the regular swings — she's on the monkey bars, hanging upside down. she dangles from the climbing structure with her hair sweeping the ground. she hangs from tree branches, from railings, from anything she can hook her legs over. other parents watch nervously while she hangs there, completely at ease, sometimes for minutes at a time.

at home, she begs me to hold her upside down by her ankles. 'flip me, mommy, flip me!' she'll drape herself backward over my lap until her hair is on the ground, and she'll stay that way while we talk or watch something together. she does headstands against the wall — not as a trick to show off, but just because. she does somersaults across the living room, one after another. she hangs off her bed with her head nearly touching the floor, and i'm constantly terrified she's going to fall and crack her head open.

it's not just that she does these things sometimes — it's like she needs them. like she's craving them. there's an intensity to it, a seeking quality. when she's been upside down for a while, she seems calmer, more centered, more able to focus. she'll sit and do a puzzle or listen to a story. but then an hour later, maybe less, she's right back at it — looking for something to hang from, something to flip over.

her teachers have mentioned that she has trouble sitting still at school. she's always moving, always fidgeting. she rocks in her chair, bounces her legs, shifts position constantly. and she tips her chair back — constantly — despite being told not to. i've caught her hanging upside down from her desk at school. the teacher was not amused.

other kids do somersaults and hang from monkey bars, i know. but this feels different. the frequency is different. the intensity is different. the need is different. it's not playing — it's seeking. she's looking for something that being upside down gives her.

i've started letting her hang upside down before school to see if it helps her sit still better. sometimes it seems to work. but i can't flip her upside down in the middle of math class. i can't let her hang from the furniture at restaurants. i don't know how to give her what she needs in ways that work in the real world.

why does my child need to be upside down so much? is this normal? is there something going on with her sensory system? and what am i supposed to do about it — stop her, let her, redirect her? she's going to be in elementary school soon and she can't hang from her desk?

acceptedAnswer.text: VESTIBULAR SEEKING BEHAVIOR / SENSORY SEEKING (VESTIBULAR) / VESTIBULAR HYPOSENSITIVITY / GRAVITATIONAL INSECURITY OPPOSITE PATTERN! Vestibular seeking refers to an intense need or craving for movement input, particularly input that stimulates the vestibular system — the sensory system located in the inner ear that detects head position and movement, providing information about balance, spatial orientation, and the body's relationship to gravity. Children who are vestibular seekers have nervous systems that require more vestibular input than typical to feel regulated, alert, and organized. Hanging upside down (inversion) provides intense vestibular stimulation by dramatically changing the head's position relative to gravity. For vestibular seekers, this input is organizing and calming rather than disorienting. They actively seek these experiences because their system craves them. This is distinct from vestibular sensitivity (gravitational insecurity), where children avoid or are distressed by movement and position changes. Vestibular seeking behavior is common in children with sensory processing differences, ADHD, autism, and other developmental variations, though it also occurs in neurotypical children with higher sensory needs. The vestibular system is foundational to sensory processing and neurological organization. Located in the inner ear, it detects head position and movement, particularly acceleration and deceleration. This information is integrated with visual and proprioceptive input to create awareness of body position in space, maintain balance, coordinate eye movements, and regulate arousal. When the vestibular system is under-responsive (hyposensitive), the brain doesn't register typical amounts of movement input, creating a craving for more intense input to achieve the same regulatory effect. Inversion (head below heart/body) provides particularly intense vestibular input because it dramatically changes the head's relationship to gravity. For vestibular seekers, this input is organizing — it helps the nervous system achieve an optimal state of alertness and regulation. Rather than trying to eliminate this seeking behavior, intervention focuses on providing appropriate vestibular input through safe, constructive channels that meet the child's sensory needs while building skills.

mainEntity.name: What is Vestibular Seeking Behavior / Sensory Seeking (Vestibular) / Vestibular Hyposensitivity / Gravitational Insecurity Opposite Pattern?

acceptedAnswer.text: Vestibular seeking refers to an intense need or craving for movement input, particularly input that stimulates the vestibular system — the sensory system located in the inner ear that detects head position and movement, providing information about balance, spatial orientation, and the body's relationship to gravity. Children who are vestibular seekers have nervous systems that require more vestibular input than typical to feel regulated, alert, and organized. Hanging upside down (inversion) provides intense vestibular stimulation by dramatically changing the head's position relative to gravity. For vestibular seekers, this input is organizing and calming rather than disorienting. They actively seek these experiences because their system craves them. This is distinct from vestibular sensitivity (gravitational insecurity), where children avoid or are distressed by movement and position changes. Vestibular seeking behavior is common in children with sensory processing differences, ADHD, autism, and other developmental variations, though it also occurs in neurotypical children with higher sensory needs. Common signs include: Frequently hangs upside down from furniture, playground equipment, or people, Seeks out swings, spinning, rolling, and tumbling activities, Appears calmer or more focused after intense movement, Tips chair back constantly, Does headstands, somersaults, cartwheels frequently.

mainEntity.name: How does a Inversion Swing / Therapy Trapeze help with vestibular seeking behavior?

acceptedAnswer.text: An inversion swing or therapy trapeze provides a designated, safe space for the child to hang upside down. This isn't about eliminating the behavior — it's about channeling it appropriately. A properly installed trapeze bar or inversion swing allows the child to hook their knees over the bar and hang safely, with controlled height, padded flooring below, and adult awareness. Some systems include a swing seat that can be used upright or inverted. The child gets exactly the vestibular input their system craves in a context designed for that purpose. Having a designated 'upside down place' can reduce the drive to hang from inappropriate places (furniture, door frames) because the need is being met. The equipment can be used as part of a sensory diet — scheduled times for inversion input throughout the day. Price: ₹2,500–12,000. DIY option: A sturdy bar installed in a doorway or ceiling with appropriate hardware can serve as a trapeze bar. Ensure professional installation and weight capacity. Use gymnastics mats or thick padding underneath. Supervise use. Playground monkey bars provide this input in outdoor settings. Safety: Installation must be secure and weight-rated. Always use padding/mats underneath. Supervise children during use. Limit inversion time to prevent blood pooling in the head (start with 30-60 seconds, observe for discomfort). Children with certain medical conditions should consult a physician before inversion activities.

mainEntity.name: How does a Platform Swing / Bolster Swing help with vestibular seeking behavior?

acceptedAnswer.text: Platform swings and bolster swings provide versatile vestibular input in multiple positions. A child can lie on their stomach with head hanging off the edge (inverted input), sit upright and swing, spin, or lie across the bolster in various positions. The swinging motion provides vestibular input, and the flexibility of positioning allows the child to find what their system needs in the moment. Spinning on a platform swing provides particularly intense vestibular input. Slow, linear swinging is more calming; fast, rotational swinging is more alerting. The child can be an active participant (pumping, spinning) or passive (being pushed), varying the intensity. These swings are staples of sensory integration therapy because they provide controlled, adjustable vestibular input across a range of intensities. Price: ₹3,000–15,000. DIY option: A sturdy board suspended by ropes from ceiling hooks can create a basic platform swing. Bolster swings can be approximated with a tightly rolled gym mat or padded cylinder suspended horizontally. Ensure secure attachment and padding below. Standard backyard swings provide linear vestibular input but less positional flexibility. Safety: Ceiling mounting requires professional installation with appropriate weight ratings. Use crash mats beneath swings. Monitor for signs of overstimulation (pallor, nausea, excessive giddiness). After intense spinning, allow time for the vestibular system to settle before the child stands up.

mainEntity.name: How does a Gymnastics Equipment (Wedges, Incline Mats, Octagon) help with vestibular seeking behavior?

acceptedAnswer.text: Gymnastics equipment provides structured opportunities for inversion and movement in controlled ways. Wedge mats support headstands and handstands, allowing the child to invert safely with their body at an angle. Incline mats create surfaces for rolling, tumbling, and transitioning through inverted positions. Octagonal barrels can be rolled over for somersaults or used as supports for inversions. Cheese mats (large wedge shapes) allow for rolling down an incline — head over heels, with the vestibular system firing with each rotation. This equipment takes the activities the child is already seeking — headstands, somersaults, flipping — and provides a proper context for them. The structured shape of the equipment guides proper positioning, reducing injury risk while maximizing the sensory input the child craves. Price: ₹2,000–20,000. DIY option: Firm couch cushions or foam blocks can support headstands. Rolled blankets can create a small barrel for rolling over. Pillows stacked in a wedge shape create an incline. A mattress on the floor provides a safe tumbling surface. These aren't as effective as proper equipment but can help meet the need. Safety: Use on appropriate surfaces (mats, carpet, grass). Supervise use. Ensure equipment is appropriately sized for the child. Teach proper technique for headstands and handstands to protect the neck. Never force inversions.

mainEntity.name: How does a Indoor Climbing Structures / Climbing Walls help with vestibular seeking behavior?

acceptedAnswer.text: Climbing provides intense proprioceptive input (muscle and joint feedback) alongside vestibular input as the body moves through space and changes position. Climbing structures and walls invite the movement seeking that vestibular cravers need. Children naturally hang from climbing structures — the bars and holds become inversion opportunities. Indoor climbing walls and structures bring this input into the home environment where it can be accessed daily, not just at the playground. Climbing also builds strength, coordination, and body awareness — all of which support the child's overall sensory-motor development. The combination of climbing (proprioceptive-vestibular) and hanging (inversion-vestibular) makes these structures particularly valuable for vestibular seekers. Price: ₹5,000–50,000+. DIY option: A sturdy ladder mounted horizontally to the wall becomes a climbing/hanging bar structure. Rope ladders can be hung from ceiling hooks. Playground visits provide climbing opportunities. Climbing trees (supervised, appropriate trees) is nature's climbing structure. Safety: Ensure structures are properly mounted and weight-rated. Use crash mats beneath climbing areas. Supervise use. Check hardware regularly. Ensure the space allows for safe falls (no sharp corners or hard surfaces in fall zone).

mainEntity.name: How does a Spinning Equipment (Sit-n-Spin, Rotational Disc, Office Chairs) help with vestibular seeking behavior?

acceptedAnswer.text: Spinning provides intense vestibular input — the rotational movement stimulates the vestibular system powerfully, which is why vestibular seekers often love it while vestibular-sensitive individuals hate it. Sit-n-spins, rotational discs, spinning tops, and even office chairs with rotating bases provide controlled spinning opportunities. The child controls the speed and duration, which is important for self-regulation. Spinning is one of the most intense forms of vestibular input available, and for seekers, it can be deeply organizing. Many children combine spinning and inversion — spinning on a disc while lying down, for example. Having spinning equipment available allows the child to access this input when needed, potentially reducing the intensity of other seeking behaviors. Price: ₹800–5,000. DIY option: An office chair with a rotating base becomes a spinning chair. A lazy Susan (large, sturdy type) can support a seated child for spinning. Even being spun by an adult (holding hands and spinning together) provides rotational input. Spinning in circles while standing is the most basic form. Safety: Monitor for signs of overstimulation — pallor, nausea, glassy eyes, excessive giddiness. Stop spinning if these occur. Allow the vestibular system to settle before standing (sit still for a moment). Some children need more time to recover from spinning than others. Spinning can be disorganizing if overdone.

mainEntity.name: How does a Yoga Ball / Therapy Ball / Peanut Ball help with vestibular seeking behavior?

acceptedAnswer.text: Therapy balls provide dynamic vestibular and proprioceptive input in versatile ways. A child can drape over the ball on their stomach (head inverted, blood flowing to head) and rock, bounce, or roll. They can sit on the ball and bounce (vestibular input while seated). They can lie across the ball in various positions. The unstable surface requires constant vestibular adjustment, providing ongoing input. Peanut balls (shaped like a peanut/hourglass) provide more stability for activities like supported inversions — the child can lie back over the ball with head toward the floor in a controlled position. These balls are portable, relatively inexpensive, and can be used in homes, classrooms, and therapy settings. Many vestibular seekers do better with ball chairs at desks because the subtle movement provides ongoing input. Price: ₹500–2,500. DIY option: Any large, sturdy inflatable ball can work. Beach balls are too soft for draping but allow for gentle bouncing. Exercise balls are widely available. Ensure the ball is properly sized for the child (when seated, feet should reach the floor, hips and knees at about 90 degrees). Safety: Ensure proper sizing. Supervise use, especially during inversions. Balls can roll unpredictably. Deflate slightly for more stability if needed. Check for punctures or weaknesses regularly. Some children need support until they develop the balance for independent use.

mainEntity.name: What are the warning signs that need professional help?

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Vestibular Seeking Behavior
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Vestibular seeking behaviors like hanging upside down reflect a nervous system that needs more vestibular input than typical to achieve optimal regulation. Rather than trying to eliminate these behaviors (which creates conflict without addressing the underlying need), intervention focuses on providing appropriate vestibular input through safe, constructive channels. When the need is met, the drive to seek in inappropriate ways diminishes.

step.name: Understand the Problem
step.text: Vestibular seeking refers to an intense need or craving for movement input, particularly input that stimulates the vestibular system — the sensory system located in the inner ear that detects head position and movement, providing information about balance, spatial orientation, and the body's relationship to gravity. Children who are vestibular seekers have nervous systems that require more vestibular input than typical to feel regulated, alert, and organized. Hanging upside down (inversion) provides intense vestibular stimulation by dramatically changing the head's position relative to gravity. For vestibular seekers, this input is organizing and calming rather than disorienting. They actively seek these experiences because their system craves them. This is distinct from vestibular sensitivity (gravitational insecurity), where children avoid or are distressed by movement and position changes. Vestibular seeking behavior is common in children with sensory processing differences, ADHD, autism, and other developmental variations, though it also occurs in neurotypical children with higher sensory needs. citation.name: Sensory Integration Theory and Vestibular Processing Research
citation.description: Research on sensory processing demonstrates that vestibular seeking behaviors reflect nervous system needs for increased vestibular input. Occupational therapy approaches based on sensory integration theory address these needs by providing appropriate vestibular input through therapeutic activities and equipment. Meeting sensory needs through appropriate channels improves self-regulation and reduces problematic seeking behaviors.

Having a designated 'upside down place' can reduce the drive to hang from inappropriate places (furniture, door frames) because the need is being met. The equipment can be used as part of a sensory diet — scheduled times for inversion input throughout the day.

Limit inversion time to prevent blood pooling in the head (start with 30-60 seconds, observe for discomfort). Children with certain medical conditions should consult a physician before inversion activities.

The swinging motion provides vestibular input, and the flexibility of positioning allows the child to find what their system needs in the moment. Spinning on a platform swing provides particularly intense vestibular input. Slow, linear swinging is more calming; fast, rotational swinging is more alerting. The child can be an active participant (pumping, spinning) or passive (being pushed), varying the intensity. These swings are staples of sensory integration therapy because they provide controlled, adjustable vestibular input across a range of intensities.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: A sturdy board suspended by ropes from ceiling hooks can create a basic platform swing. Bolster swings can be approximated with a tightly rolled gym mat or padded cylinder suspended horizontally. Ensure secure attachment and padding below. Standard backyard swings provide linear vestibular input but less positional flexibility.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ceiling mounting requires professional installation with appropriate weight ratings. Use crash mats beneath swings. Monitor for signs of overstimulation (pallor, nausea, excessive giddiness). After intense spinning, allow time for the vestibular system to settle before the child stands up.

item.name: Gymnastics Equipment (Wedges, Incline Mats, Octagon)
item.description: Gymnastics equipment provides structured opportunities for inversion and movement in controlled ways. Wedge mats support headstands and handstands, allowing the child to invert safely with their body at an angle. Incline mats create surfaces for rolling, tumbling, and transitioning through inverted positions. Octagonal barrels can be rolled over for somersaults or used as supports for inversions. Cheese mats (large wedge shapes) allow for rolling down an incline — head over heels, with the vestibular system firing with each rotation. This equipment takes the activities the child is already seeking — headstands, somersaults, flipping — and provides a proper context for them. The structured shape of the equipment guides proper positioning, reducing injury risk while maximizing the sensory input the child craves.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Firm couch cushions or foam blocks can support headstands. Rolled blankets can create a small barrel for rolling over. Pillows stacked in a wedge shape create an incline. A mattress on the floor provides a safe tumbling surface. These aren't as effective as proper equipment but can help meet the need.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use on appropriate surfaces (mats, carpet, grass). Supervise use. Ensure equipment is appropriately sized for the child. Teach proper technique for headstands and handstands to protect the neck. Never force inversions.

item.name: Indoor Climbing Structures / Climbing Walls
item.description: Climbing provides intense proprioceptive input (muscle and joint feedback) alongside vestibular input as the body moves through space and changes position. Climbing structures and walls invite the movement seeking that vestibular cravers need. Children naturally hang from climbing structures — the bars and holds become inversion opportunities. Indoor climbing walls and structures bring this input into the home environment where it can be accessed daily, not just at the playground. Climbing also builds strength, coordination, and body awareness — all of which support the child's overall sensory-motor development. The combination of climbing (proprioceptive-vestibular) and hanging (inversion-vestibular) makes these structures particularly valuable for vestibular seekers.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: A sturdy ladder mounted horizontally to the wall becomes a climbing/hanging bar structure. Rope ladders can be hung from ceiling hooks. Playground visits provide climbing opportunities. Climbing trees (supervised, appropriate trees) is nature's climbing structure.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure structures are properly mounted and weight-rated. Use crash mats beneath climbing areas. Supervise use. Check hardware regularly. Ensure the space allows for safe falls (no sharp corners or hard surfaces in fall zone).

item.name: Spinning Equipment (Sit-n-Spin, Rotational Disc, Office Chairs)
item.description: Spinning provides intense vestibular input — the rotational movement stimulates the vestibular system powerfully, which is why vestibular seekers often love it while vestibular-sensitive individuals hate it. Sit-n-spins, rotational discs, spinning tops, and even office chairs with rotating bases provide controlled spinning opportunities. The child controls the speed and duration, which is important for self-regulation. Spinning is one of the most intense forms of vestibular input available, and for seekers, it can be deeply organizing. Many children combine spinning and inversion — spinning on a disc while lying down, for example. Having spinning equipment available allows the child to access this input when needed, potentially reducing the intensity of other seeking behaviors.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: An office chair with a rotating base becomes a spinning chair. A lazy Susan (large, sturdy type) can support a seated child for spinning. Even being spun by an adult (holding hands and spinning together) provides rotational input. Spinning in circles while standing is the most basic form.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Monitor for signs of overstimulation — pallor, nausea, glassy eyes, excessive giddiness. Stop spinning if these occur. Allow the vestibular system to settle before standing (sit still for a moment). Some children need more time to recover from spinning than others. Spinning can be disorganizing if overdone.

item.name: Yoga Ball / Therapy Ball / Peanut Ball
item.description: Therapy balls provide dynamic vestibular and proprioceptive input in versatile ways. A child can drape over the ball on their stomach (head inverted, blood flowing to head) and rock, bounce, or roll. They can sit on the ball and bounce (vestibular input while seated). They can lie across the ball in various positions. The unstable surface requires constant vestibular adjustment, providing ongoing input. Peanut balls (shaped like a peanut/hourglass) provide more stability for activities like supported inversions — the child can lie back over the ball with head toward the floor in a controlled position. These balls are portable, relatively inexpensive, and can be used in homes, classrooms, and therapy settings. Many vestibular seekers do better with ball chairs at desks because the subtle movement provides ongoing input.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Any large, sturdy inflatable ball can work. Beach balls are too soft for draping but allow for gentle bouncing. Exercise balls are widely available. Ensure the ball is properly sized for the child (when seated, feet should reach the floor, hips and knees at about 90 degrees).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure proper sizing. Supervise use, especially during inversions. Balls can roll unpredictably. Deflate slightly for more stability if needed. Check for punctures or weaknesses regularly. Some children need support until they develop the balance for independent use.

item.name: Hammock Swings / Cocoon Swings
item.description: Hammock swings and cocoon swings provide swinging vestibular input while allowing for position changes within the swing. A child can sit upright, lie down, curl up, or — importantly for vestibular seekers — position themselves with head lower than feet. The fabric of the hammock/cocoon creates a contained, secure feeling while allowing the movement the child craves. Cocoon swings especially provide deep pressure input alongside vestibular input, which can be particularly regulating for children who seek both. The swinging can be gentle and calming or more intense depending on the child's needs in the moment. These swings are often favorites for calming down, transitioning, or sensory breaks.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: A sturdy fabric hammock can be hung from ceiling hooks or a stand. A large piece of stretchy fabric (therapy lycra) suspended from two points creates a simple cocoon swing. Ensure hanging hardware is rated for the child's weight plus movement forces. Test all knots and connections.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Secure installation is essential — swings create dynamic forces beyond just the child's weight. Ensure fabric is strong and stitching is secure. Check hardware and fabric regularly. Supervise use. Ensure adequate clearance for swinging range.

item.name: Balance Equipment (Balance Boards, Wobble Cushions)
item.description: Balance equipment provides ongoing, subtle vestibular input by creating unstable surfaces that require constant vestibular adjustment. Balance boards (rocker boards, wobble boards, balance discs) challenge the vestibular system to make continuous small corrections to maintain balance. Wobble cushions placed on chairs turn sitting into a vestibular activity — the child must constantly adjust to the moving surface. For vestibular seekers, this ongoing low-level input can be regulating and can reduce the drive for more intense seeking behaviors. Balance equipment can be used during seated activities (wobble cushion on chair during homework) or during active play (standing on balance boards). The input is less intense than inversion or spinning but more sustainable throughout the day.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: A piece of wood over a half-circle creates a basic rocker board. A partially deflated ball or pillow under a board creates an unstable surface. Cushions with some give work as simple wobble seats. Walking along curbs or low walls provides balance challenges during everyday activities.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Start with less challenging equipment and progress. Use near a support (wall, furniture) initially. Ensure the floor isn't slippery. Some balance boards can tip dramatically — supervise initial use and teach safe mounting/dismounting.

item.name: Scooter Boards
item.description: Scooter boards are simple platforms on wheels that children can ride in various positions — sitting, kneeling, or prone (lying on stomach). The prone position is particularly valuable for vestibular seekers because the head can be held up (neck extension) or hang down (inversion) while the body moves through space. Propelling the scooter board with the arms provides proprioceptive input alongside the vestibular input of movement. Scooter board activities can include navigating obstacle courses, racing, playing games, or just zooming around. The versatility and fun factor make scooter boards highly motivating. They're staples in OT clinics and can be used at home in appropriate spaces (smooth floors, adequate room).

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: A smooth board with four caster wheels creates a basic scooter board. Ensure wheels spin freely and the board is sturdy enough for the child's weight. Carpet squares or furniture movers can work on very smooth floors but offer less control.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use on smooth, clear surfaces only. Fingers and hair can get caught in wheels — teach safe hand and head positioning. The child can fall off — use on padded surfaces or low-pile carpet. Never use near stairs or inclines unless in a controlled, supervised setting.

socialMedia.caption: When your child hangs upside down constantly — from furniture, from playground bars, from you — they're not misbehaving. Their vestibular system is seeking the input it needs to feel regulated. This is sensory seeking, and fighting it doesn't work. Meet the need instead. Here are 9 materials that help: inversion swings/trapeze bars (safe places to hang), platform and bolster swings, gymnastics equipment for rolling and flipping, climbing structures, spinning equipment, therapy balls, hammock swings, balance equipment, and scooter boards. Give them what their nervous system craves, safely and appropriately. Save for your upside-down kiddo! 🔖 #VestibularSeeking #SensoryProcessing #PinnacleBlooms
